# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a simple Dockerfile for our Go microservice. Compile the application and run the binary. Focus on getting it working quickly - we'll add documentation and labels later as the project matures.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Golang runtime image based off alpine linux (alpine) with go installed, using a specific tag for our github repo branch of interest (.net/master or any other tags available on your repository).  This ensures we are running the most recent version.  
FROM scratch @test --ulimit nofile=512:64000 -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock || true #Remove this for testing, or use a different runtime to be able capture logs from the container and save them into ./.docker
# Set environment variable – Required in Go Languages such as GOOS(Operating system) & GOOSSERVICE (Service Name).   It’s necessary because Docker images are typically designed for one operating system at a time.    If you were using multi-platform docker, it would be best to not use this or provide different versions of your application in the multiplatform format as they will end up with 2 separate containers
ENV GOOS=linux   GOOSSERVICE myAppService    	       #Docker Image is designed for Linux OS and uses 'myAppServices' tag. You can replace any name you want, but need to be unique in docker registry/container repository
# Copy our .go binary file (your app) into the container image directory   - required so that your application runs correctly inside a Docker Container – Use absolute path if necessary otherwise use relative paths as much more common.    It’s best practice for us to not include any data or directories in docker images which do no require these, using .docker and /var/run are usually fine
COPY ./myApp ${GOOS} --no-cache   # Use absolute path if necessary otherwise use relative paths as much more common. – Required because when your application runs inside Docker Container the directory '/home' of where you run docker container has to be an volume in such case, else it will fail
# Set environment variable for any GoLang app - This is usually used while running go build ./myAppCommand or if ./.docker file exists.   It’s necessary because Docker images are typically designed with application code and settings inside the image that'd be required to run an exact same setup on your local system in a different environment such as development, staging etc.. 
ENV APP_VERSION=0.1    # Required for go build or other tools like grunt / gulp which might call this script while running application inside Docker Container - it’s necessary because each run of docker image could require something unique to identify the same setup and environment ie, different version number
# Run any command on container'start – Use 'cmd', you can use your app requirements.   It is where all go-languages code will be put inside a Docker Container at start up process so that users get an identical working experience every time they run the same image - Can include commands to setup data, initialize services etc
WORKDIR ${GOOS}  # This sets your application's root directory.   It’ll contain all necessary files for our app including '.go', './myApp'.    For Linux images '.' should be set as working dir and also used in Go command -GOPATH must remain constant from one run to the next, so that users get a consistent work environment
# Build binary with go build flag using ./.docker file – If any commands are required under your app requirements this will come into play.    It’s necessary because when you install Docker and use it in another setup on other machine or docker-machine then need to execute these command at least once so that those settings works for every new instance of image
RUN go build -o myApp ${GOOS}  # Use absolute path if needed otherwise provide relative paths.   It’s used by tools like grunt/gulp which might call this script while running application inside Docker Container to ensure same setup is maintained across machines where the container runs on or a docker-machine run
# Expose port using -p flag – This allows your app listening for incoming requests.   It’s necessary because you want users access points (port) outside of current working directory in such case, as it'll be used to get inside our application and start up process at running the docker container with same ./.docker file
EXPOSE ${PORT}  # Required by Docker run command for port mapping - When you setup a network then this is mandatory.   It’s necessary because when users want them enter points of service they use to point where your application should be accessible and we need information about it from outside, via the container or using ports
# Run app inside docker – This will allow our microservice/app starting up at startup process - When Docker run is called then this command needs execution for that instance.   It’s necessary because when users want to start their applications in other environments you need a single setup call and be able execute the application, so it can ensure same work environment inside different machine or docker-machine
CMD ["myApp"]  # Required by Docker run command as this sets up default settings for your app - When someone calls myapp on port provided then start that process.   It’s necessary because when they want to call the application within running container in other environment, at least one action needs be done or set like setting a web server address and so forth inside Docker run command
